speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 11. We're interviewing Dr. William Perl. I had one question that I wanted to ask you, and then I'll pick up where we left off a minute ago. Where were you when you learned Germany had surrendered?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, on that day-- in-- first of all, when Hitler was dead, when he committed suicide, I was only in a castle, just overnight sleeping on the way from one prisoner of war camp to another one. And it was near [INAUDIBLE]. I think it was [INAUDIBLE]. And the prisoner of war-- then-- the-- the-- a short time after that, I was in [INAUDIBLE] when I learned that Germany had surrendered unconditionally.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you were telling me about one of the McCarthy investigations and how you were very much being investigated because of your role in this particular investigation of the execution of the American prisoners of war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was-- not one of them was healthy. [INAUDIBLE] There came a a commission from Germany, a [INAUDIBLE], a royal, whatever his name was, who was very eager to establish friendship with the Germans. And in the [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE] was involved, the commanding general of the Army [INAUDIBLE]. And he had been Hitler's-- that's what we heard. Hitler's sergeants in World War I and had been his bouncer in the meetings, in the early meetings. And he was very much down [INAUDIBLE] by the generals. But he was involved into this case. Because through his hands passed Hitler's order not to make any prisoners during this war. [INAUDIBLE]. He-- he didn't say it like that, Hitler. Hitler said, we will-- we have to advance with-- without showing any [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] weakness. No, he didn't say weakness. The-- the-- the-- the knowledge of our acting like [INAUDIBLE] has to precede our troops. And he didn't say exactly prisoners of war will be made. But he-- as it came down, it was that prisoners were not to be made. And-- but the only general I interviewed in this position was [INAUDIBLE], with whom I got a complete confession. You see, the idea-- it was quite a split opinion in the American public, which was very interesting. The American system of justice should be fair and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And even if they would have been beaten, what was that compared to what they did? The unit which committed this crime was the first SS sponsor [INAUDIBLE] of the [INAUDIBLE] Adolf Hitler, Hitler's body-- bodyguard division. It was the toughest of the toughest.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you cannot expect these people to be so, let's say-- let's say evil. They're tough. And if I would have used violence, I would have done it [INAUDIBLE] because this is what they were prepared for. They were ready to die and to do everything for their cause. Well, I approached them not with violence as McCarthy claimed, but with what they called tricks, psychological tricks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you stationed during this investigation? Where did this take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: This took place in-- in-- in Schwaebich Hall. Schwaebich, S-C-H-W-A-E-B-I-C-H. Hall, H-A-L-L. There was a big penitentiary there. And there were men who work [INAUDIBLE] empty [INAUDIBLE] German [INAUDIBLE]. And we were all prisoners there.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I believe it started-- in Schwaebich Hall. It started in-- in about-- about Christmas time of 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the trial was in the spring of 1946. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a big movement in Germany supported by the clergy that-- to let these people free. Except [INAUDIBLE], he was a-- was married to a daughter of a beer baron, of one of the biggest breweries in Germany. And he was very influential. And the clergy in Germany was very Nazi anyhow, many north of them. So they supported this demand for a new trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you involved in any other war crimes trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but not so essentially. Besides, before it was put in [INAUDIBLE], I was involved in finding and bringing into prisoners of war camps if I can establish where-- where they are, people who had taken part in the beatings [INAUDIBLE] shooting of American flyers. American flyers, when they parachuted, where they were all killed. There was an order after this from the gestapo, from the [INAUDIBLE], from the SS. They had order to kill him. And I went into the little villages to inquire where-- we knew where the-- where the planes had run down to find out. And I established especially who was involved in [INAUDIBLE] into American [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't follow it up anymore. One place was very funny. I came to a place. I don't remember the name of the town-- a little place about 60 or 80 miles northwest of Frankfurt where flyers had gone down. And we assumed that they are being killed because they was not [INAUDIBLE]. So I went to the burgermeister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And by that time, they had gotten rid of the Nazis and then put in power others, supposedly anti-Nazi. Sometimes, this was right. Sometimes, they were [INAUDIBLE] Nazis [? still. ?] And eventually, the burgermeister told you, I understand that a flyer was hit here. He says, yeah. I said, you got data from him? And then I asked him, what happened to the man who hit him? They said, the man who killed him is-- is dead. I said, how do you know? I mean, what's the proof that he is dead? He said, he was the only chief of the local police. And he shot him. I said, who can-- who knows about that? Any of the [INAUDIBLE] knows about it because everybody [INAUDIBLE]. Said, all the Poles know about it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- and he said he was killed by a Pole. By-- he was killed by a Pollack. Because he [INAUDIBLE] according to the Polish [INAUDIBLE], at the end of the war, he was killed by a Pollack.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was-- I didn't trust him. So I says, who is this Pollack? And he gave me the name of one of the guys [INAUDIBLE] Polish worker. And it was a big excitement when the [INAUDIBLE] war crimes in the town. And I said, I want to speak to this man who is supposed to have killed [INAUDIBLE] the police officer who [INAUDIBLE] killed the American flyer. And the [? chief ?] of police, I said, yes, I killed him. I says, why did you kill him? He said, because he was a Nazi. He was a German, he said. I said, is this-- was it for real to shoot him? He said, I had permission to kill him. Imagine permission to kill him. I said, tell me in detail what happened. He says, well, he-- we knew that he had killed Polish or beaten up brutally Polish slave workers and that he had shot this American prisoner when we landed. And so I killed him. I said, what happened to him? He said he was-- no-- what happened to this man whom you killed? Said, he was dead. I said, what's the proof that you had the [? man's ?] permission? So he spoke that he was already prepared for it and had a little piece of paper, scrap paper, on which was-- which was given by the man, the first man who [INAUDIBLE] the first tanks which entered the city. And it read, OK, kill the bastard. Kill the bastard. The name for the guy, PFC. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they killed him. But I personally knew many of them [INAUDIBLE]. These were my-- my-- this is before when I was assigned to [INAUDIBLE]. Were you ever involved in the Nuremberg Trials?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was in the-- the Nuremberg trials were only-- only for the biggest big shots. All the others were tried in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you involved in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Dachau trial-- the Dachau trial-- the Rosenberg trial was in Dachau. The [INAUDIBLE] were all in Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that-- and that's where you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: As well there. But I was very-- I had very little to do with them because I was fully busy with the [INAUDIBLE] case. It was by far the most important one for the Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to change direction for just a moment. During the time that you were in the United States and with the American military, did you have any contact with your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I-- I didn't hear her, but I knew she was in a concentration camp. She wrote me a note that she-- that she-- and she was first ones, you know, before she was arrested. And she continued her activity. And she said, I had-- I had visitors when I came from so-and-so. And-- and I-- but-- then later, I'm looking forward to see [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was the kind of man who has been brought to Dachau and to Buchenwald. And in our courts, this [INAUDIBLE] stood for concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew this is-- is most likely the concentration camp. I was very much upset when I heard from a prisoner for the first time that they used women to-- for the-- for the [? birthers, ?] for the soldiers [? birthers. ?] But they didn't use Jewish women for this. But she didn't-- wasn't considered Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: They did not know that she had converted. And others they made an experiment with making some notes. Able to have seen her anymore-- I was very much upset by that. Because I knew that she would stand up and stand out. Yes. And I wrote to her. I wrote to her via a friend of ours in Switzerland. I had a friend who was in a fraternity similar to the one to which I belong. [INAUDIBLE] fraternity in Zurich. And I wrote to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and enclosed in envelope was the letter to Lore. The sender was Willie Wilhelm [? Lintpoint. ?] Now, [? Lintpoint, ?] that's about as non-Jewish a name as Murphy not-- not Italian, you know? And I wrote the letter, and she put my letter into this envelope and put a [INAUDIBLE] stamp on it. And then she wrote a letter, received from Wilhelm [? Lintpoint ?] to Lore. She got a-- she got the letters from me. So she knew you were in America.

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- she knew that I was in America. She-- she did not know that I was in the army. I thought it's better to-- the less she knows, the better it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your finding her again after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was my greatest experience, my happiest day. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our unit is very well. We have all European Jews, and we're-- we're interrogating war crimes, and we have very good results. And we were a top class unit in the intelligence. And suddenly, the production fell very much. And morale was very low because of the stupid non-fraternization rule. There was a rule not to fraternize with the German-- you-- you couldn't even say hello to anybody. [INAUDIBLE] to keep completely away from them. And the less the officer, command officer, knew about the Germans, the harder the enforce is. And our people wanted to find out what happened to their families, wanted to go to their hometown and find out their-- and they couldn't go because they were not supposed to speak to Germans. [INAUDIBLE] our commanding officer, Major [INAUDIBLE], was a member of the Philadelphia [INAUDIBLE] old family group that has given into our-- in fact, our whole unit thought that we should have some representation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you got all European Jews so that somebody [INAUDIBLE]. It was important you spoke the right way of English. And he was called by his head [INAUDIBLE] said, what's happened to your-- to your unit? He said they don't know. Production went down so much. And he called me. And maybe he asked others too about what's going on. Do you know this? I said, I'm very aware of this. Well, he said, oh, real-- real loner, works alone [INAUDIBLE]. I told him about it. And I said, what shall-- shall we do? What shall we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, that will give us permission to look in our home towns for our relatives. So he gave permission for-- for two jeeps to leave. One left for Theresienstadt, which was occupied by the-- by American troops in Bohemia. Terezin, which was a kind of concentration camp, but it was a very mild concentration camp. It was more a detention camp and a collection point to send to Auschwitz. And the other one was just-- I want to go to Vienna. He had no idea that there was a strict-- a very strict rule to keep away from the Russian zone. The-- the borders had not been established yet, and Americans were afraid of-- of diplomatic complications. But the GIs who might be [INAUDIBLE], might be a [INAUDIBLE], and so on. And one, it was a big place, that was the case. One journalist who traveled against the regulations to Berlin and interviewed people there. And he was arrested, and he was sent back. And this was a big story. [INAUDIBLE] no knowledge [INAUDIBLE] seen the Russian [INAUDIBLE]. I don't think I spoke of Vienna. I spoke of Austria.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- he said, I can't give you [INAUDIBLE]. I said, [INAUDIBLE] I have to look in various places for people in Austria. So he gave me an order which I have here [INAUDIBLE] here and to go with the first lieutenant, [? Francios ?] [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE], one of the oldest Austrian families. It's aristocratic.

speaker: interviewee
text: The parents have [INAUDIBLE] have been prime ministers of Austria and so on. And he had to be very much for the independence of Austria. And then [INAUDIBLE] came in. He went-- and I went in his jeep to Austria and [INAUDIBLE] what a Jewish [INAUDIBLE] with relatives, his parents in Vienna. And he was supposed to go to Austria. And he didn't notice we-- we were going to the Russian zone too, that we intend to. We took off early in the morning and-- well, in the afternoon-- and they [INAUDIBLE] a beautiful [INAUDIBLE] when [INAUDIBLE] had a card for their relatives. And these people-- we stayed with them overnight. And these people [INAUDIBLE] not to enter the Russian zone. It's a danger which was really dangerous, you know what, having to [INAUDIBLE] back. They arrest you [INAUDIBLE] whether you are an American or not. You will disappear. But I went to Vienna anyhow. I made up a passport of my own in English [INAUDIBLE]. Not a passport-- a document in English and in Russian [INAUDIBLE] in our prisoner, a Russian who had worked with the German collaborators and was arrested there. And he-- he immediately [INAUDIBLE] said the holder of this document-- I had one for all three and one for each individual. Second Lieutenant William Perl is proceeding into Russian-- into Russian occupied territory on an important mission or allied personnel. [INAUDIBLE] [? asked ?] to be-- to this allied officer of utmost help. So then when I came, I didn't go through the main checking point [INAUDIBLE]. There was a main-- main point [? for-- ?] and once I go a little further south [INAUDIBLE]. And there-- there was on the bridge with the entry where on one side was an American guard. The other side was Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the guys told me [INAUDIBLE] then they shot him in the heart. They also [INAUDIBLE] military intelligence. And I told him what-- what-- what do you have to do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, [INAUDIBLE] confidential military [INAUDIBLE]. Those became [INAUDIBLE] all about. the Russians. And I-- first of all, we-- we had loaded our jeep with all kinds of goodies for us, whiskey and cigarettes. And we distributed a ton of it. And one of the Russians jumped into the jeep and says, go. I told you guys go now. To my horror, he drove up to the commander [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] they asked us [INAUDIBLE]. And it appears. And soon, he came back with that officer and the same lieutenant. And this officer-- none of them spoke English, but at least that man spoke German. Many of the Russians spoke German. The lieutenant spoke German too.

speaker: interviewee
text: They had been fighting the Germans for a long time. The Russians are very talented for languages. And they addressed me in German. Of course, I didn't understand a word of German. I would have made myself suspicious. An American who knows German? Not-- not a [? straight ?] story. But we found-- we found out that you can communicate because I understand Czech. I was born Prague. And he-- he-- he asked me, what do you want? Where are you going? And I told him, I am on a confidential mission. I cannot tell you. And he said, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he came back and said, you are going to [INAUDIBLE]. The main general [INAUDIBLE] and to f now in order to negotiate about the entrance of Americans in [INAUDIBLE]. I said, I cannot tell you. He says, I know you are going to [INAUDIBLE]. And he was-- this lieutenant was assigned to accompany us in the jeep to [INAUDIBLE] where the American general was. There-- [INAUDIBLE] they tried to telephone [INAUDIBLE] but didn't work. The telephone didn't work. And of course, if there was one place I-- one person I didn't want to see was this American general. And then we started drinking, and he became inebriated. He drank one, and I drank one. And then he drank one. And the other one [INAUDIBLE]. There are only two [INAUDIBLE] coming on. [INAUDIBLE] and then he-- he got more inebriated. We didn't drink anymore. He drank alone. And he told us how beautiful Vienna was and all the experiences which he had with women in Vienna, which has [INAUDIBLE] of women. And I said-- I-- I wouldn't talk [? that time. ?] So directed him to tell me-- to suggest, let's go and see Vienna. Or maybe I told him, let's go and see Vienna. He says, yes. We went to Vienna. And we drove down the street. And I saw a girl with a baby in her arm. And she looked at me like [INAUDIBLE] had been her last ever. And I [? said, ?] to the woman, says, [INAUDIBLE]. She has a baby from somebody else. I said, [INAUDIBLE], don't make any difference to me. But with me, [? she's harmless. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came to another house was bombed out. The windows were-- no glass is but wooden [INAUDIBLE]. And I knocked at the door. And there was-- who was answering, [? anybody ?] don't know. I ask, I want to speak to Lore [? Rollig. ?] And she said, she isn't here right now. I said, where is she? She said, she left in the afternoon. She had some errand. Now at least she's alive. She got here. And when-- when I came out, when I spoke to her, Lore, where did you go? See him running down the street.

speaker: interviewee
text: She has been together-- the guy who was standing with her was a son of a woman who had been her teacher. And she said, when the jeep passed, she said, these [? aren't Russians. ?] And she said, no, these aren't Russians. These are either Americans or British. And she said, Americans, that's for me. And she run down. She had so much confidence it was me. She was all packed. She was all packed because she knew within two weeks of the surrender, I will come. And it took longer. It was six weeks. But I came in the [INAUDIBLE] unfortunately found out that his parents had been deported. And she left the same evening to Vienna. It was a fascinating trip. In the light of the headlights of the jeep, you could see these Russian vehicles, some truck, and some horse-drawn with its small Russian horses and women sitting there next to the driver and all had the fantastic attire. [INAUDIBLE] there's drapes everywhere. And we are wrapped into them. And we came to the border. And at the border, I got through the [INAUDIBLE] the lieutenant who covered us. We didn't want any trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by-- by then, he knew that there was something wrong. But we were through. And then he asked me, where-- where the hell are you coming from, Lieutenant? And I said, we come from a confidential mission. He says, I can't let you. I says, why? And he said, I let nobody in and nobody out-- no exceptions. I said, what do you suggest we do? He said, you wait until-- until the officer or somebody else comes in. That was 2:00 or 3:00 in the morning. It was June. And the-- I started to-- the car started to get away. I said, so you make a fool out of ourselves.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Russians let us out, and you let the jeep with two American personnel stand here. Though he was doubtful. And he looked into the [INAUDIBLE]. Says, you have a civilian here too. I said, sure. We-- we had the mission.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said [? welcome, ?] and we came to arrest her. So he says, I can't let you through. I said, what [INAUDIBLE] to get us through? He says, I would have to communicate with lieutenant so and so. He was the sergeant. And I said, what's going on here? How do you communicate? He didn't have the [INAUDIBLE] speaker. He says, I have to shoot. I said, OK, shoot. If you want to have him-- wake him up at 3:00 in the morning, he'll sure appreciate it. He asked me, are-- are you intending to stay in the town, or are you going on? I said, we are going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went on and [INAUDIBLE] into the American territory. That was the 22nd of June when we celebrated our wedding day. Because we had married before, but we could never live together. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in the American army after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army? Until I was discharged. My-- my points had come up earlier. I could have earlier but I was [INAUDIBLE] the persecution until about September 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's when you left the army, the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't yet. I went-- I went to the discharge paper. But I stayed [INAUDIBLE] has plenty of leave coming. I was still on leave until November or December was the [INAUDIBLE] December and joined to [INAUDIBLE]. And I sit in the reserves. And when the Korean War started-- when the Korean War started, I-- I was to be called up-- that I was informed that you will be called up to-- to go to Eisenhower's headquarters in Versailles in Paris because I spoke very [INAUDIBLE] US intelligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me, I have to interrupt you because we have to change tape. Just one moment.
